speaker: interviewer
text: February 7th 1995. Survivor, Michael Kaplan. Tape three.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, I come in through that place that that sergeant over there from the-- he was-- he was the master of that-- of that repair shop. He told me I should come in to him study So I said, you have to give me a paper to come study. Because in the morning when I get out, they're catching people in the street. And so I won't be able to come. So he said, fine. So he went to the office [NON-ENGLISH] and printed out a pass that nobody should touch me. I'm working there in this place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, we worked until the end of 1941. I used to come every day there to work. Over there, we were treated more or less like humane. Yeah, I got a kick here and there. Not that-- this was, you know, nothing special, nothing unusual. And we worked there. One day, we came in the morning and-- we came in the morning and they told us to-- we knew that the front went already further. And any day, they should close up here and-- and move up. So one day, we come-- we coming in the morning. There was a clothing truck, and on the floor were plenty of potatoes. They told us to go in there, the three of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got in there and they took us all the way to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the company is supposed to be quartered in Vinnitsa. But on the way, they stopped in [NON-ENGLISH] This was already deep in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In [NON-ENGLISH], we got out form the truck. They let us out. We ate something or something like that. And there were Russian people coming over and talking around me. We spoke Russian. So we asked them, where are we? They said, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's Russia already. So that's how we found out, we got-- they took us all the way into [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: But then later on, after a while, we got back into the truck and they went all the way to Vinnitsa. In Vinnitsa, there was already part of this-- these people-- part of the German company which prepared for us, for them, the quarters. In there, we worked. We had garages. It was an abandoned post office, I think it was. They had the base for trucks and all these kind of things. And there we worked. We worked the whole winter-- fixed trucks, cleaned them, and welding, all this kind of-- all these kind of things-- the dirty work, nothing washing the-- if you took out a motor from the truck, we washed it with the petrol so they can work on it. And there we worked with them till-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] till spring, 1941-- no, spring 1941, right. At that time, the front already passed Kiev. The Germans took Kiev and advanced to Kiev. So the whole thing got folded up again, the whole operation. And they moved-- and they moved to Kiev-- not really into the town. They moved in-- in Sviatoshino. This is before Kiev, 10 kilometers before Kiev. So they-- they settled down in a school. And for us, they gave us a few like shacks, little houses that we settled there. And interesting part is, when we were in-- in-- in-- in Vinnitsa, we tried to get in contact with-- we found out that when they're fixing the railroad, the trains-- not the railroad, the-- in the city, they're riding around in the-- the-- tram, [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the passenger cars, but were going by rail. We tried to get in touch with them to how about to escape and go into the partisans. They wouldn't take us because we were Jewish. So this one-- one-- one try, we didn't succeed.

speaker: interviewer
text: While being in-- in-- outside Kiev, from time to time, they took us to-- into town to-- to bathe, and to wash ourself. And there we try to also ask people, how-- how can we get into partisans? Because we knew that the end will be, they're going to get-- they're going to get rid of us. They're going to kill us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody wants you, because we were Jewish-- again, the same story. But while we were working there in-- in-- in Sviatoshino, there was-- the front went further. And-- and somehow, they knew they were going to settle for a longer time. So the chef of this repair shop figured out he's going to make a shower for the Germans there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So across the street were some houses. He took us along and we should look around there. We get some pipe. We need pipes for the showers, they wanted build for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, we're finding there's a shaft going down into the ground about 200 feet. They let-- one of us they let down by a rope to see what-- to get some pipes. Pipes were coming out from that shaft. While being down there, we found out there-- that there's hallways, to the left, to the right. There was a communication center. There were beds from a hospital left over in a hurry, in bunks, all these kind of things. Then when we came up, we told, hey, there's a whole town downstairs. There's three way out, and all the way hallways, long way. The German got curious. He went down and start looking around, and this is what it was. The Russian built the underground communication bunker. It was five miles one way, five miles the other way-- three prong communication [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bunker. And we dismantled all the pipes that we-- that we need, and we-- we-- we build out the shower for-- for the Germans. There were other cases that the Germans used to go out, and they need to-- there was one in German in particular. He looked for paintings. So he took us, or sometimes, he took me, sometimes, he took another guy to be his translator. Because they didn't speak any Russian. So we were translating for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while we were translating for them, we asked the guy, hey, how about us to-- we can go to the partisans? You know, to-- he said, no. We don't take any Jews. So we-- we were dumped right there. We had no-- no-- no choice to just hang around with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, the-- the front went forward. And from Kiev, they moved in to Zaporozh'e. Zaporozh'e, this is under [NON-ENGLISH]. And they moved to Zaporozh'e, They settled us in an apartment house. And us they gave a little house on the side. So we-- we-- we were there. And we fixed it up, because the windows were broken, and they had to be fixed up and everything. And every day, we went to work with them. And while we were there, Russian girls used to come to-- to wash the Germans' clothes, to fix it, to clean the rooms. So somehow, we got in-- in-- in touch with them, and we spoke to them. Look, we are here. We would like to run away, and this kind of things. And the same thing-- they said, we have no-- no room for you in the partisans. And this was already about 19-- and we worked steady. Every day, we worked on the trucks, under the, trucks fixing, all these kind of things. There was this guy that-- a friend of mine which he lives in Montreal. After work, we could go out. Right-- right there close-- close by was another-- not a village, like a family. A few families lived there. So they were hungry, going hungry these people. So in the trucks, the Germans used to bring in wheat. I don't know what they intend to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stole two, three kilo of wheat, and in the evening, he wanted to go there and give it to them they should have what to eat. One German soldier caught him. And he-- and he got 15 or 20 on his behind. And they put him in-- in a frozen bathroom. And he stood there for a week. But what did we do? Since I was left with my other friend, which lives down in Israel, we used to get from the girls milk. When they used to come every day to work, the girls, used to get the milk, warm up the milk on an electric stove-- we made it ourselves-- and warm up the milk and go a floor above and lower down the bottle of milk. And Jakob picked up the milk, and he was drinking hot milk. That's how he could sustain better the cold. How did you-- what we were living conditions like there? You worked every day and then you came home to-- to what? Where were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: The living condition was-- you know, we had a-- we had a stove there. Because it was a time that as soon as you moved in, you were looking around to have-- have a stove. So there was-- there were looking around and was left over something we could take it for ourselves and then-- and put in a stove. You make a pipe, you know, somehow. To the window you let out the pipe. And that's how we had-- we had a stove.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about sleeping quarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] We had beds or we slept on the floor. Straw and all this kind of thing, everything was laying around just for the taking.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mattresses-- we were three mechanics, and-- and a tailor, and-- and a shoemaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were five of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five of-- five of us. So he-- he went-- after when he was released from that-- that prison there-- that bathroom-- because he was locked in from the outside-- he-- we worked there. And the girls used to come every time. So it was a time in-- in the beginning of 1943, I think it was, yeah. 1943, the Russians start to advance already. They start to advance and came to a point that they were about five kilometers from the place where we were quartered. And we heard the-- the docking and all those kind of things. And the Germans say, oh, the Russians are coming. The Russians are coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for us, it was fine. The Russians are coming. They were going to liberate us. We decided to escape that time. Because how long you take it? Another five kilometers to go for the Russians so we be free? It didn't work that way. It didn't work out that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: We decided to run away at lunchtime. We made an offer the girls. I'll go to this girl, and my friend will go to this girl, and the other girl, and the-- the-- the tailor will go there. And we run away lunchtime, And we got at our places. We figure, well, another day, we'd be gone. Happens so that the Germans pushed back the Russians. And we got stuck in between. We got stuck in between a week, two weeks. This is the field police, like the MP on the frontier. And the partisans became very active there. Because the Russians were close by, the partisans became very active. So there, these police were looking for partisans-- partisans that didn't have any uniforms. They walked around in plain clothing. So the-- the people that were hiding us, they-- they say, look here. The Germans are looking for-- for-- for partisans. We cannot keep you here. Because once they-- they catch us, all of us are going to be killed. And we didn't put them-- we didn't want to put them in-- in-- in a position that they're going to be killed for us. If they catch us alone, they kill us because we are Jewish. But they're just-- so we decided-- to the partisans we couldn't go. So they told us that we should go back about 200 kilometers, 150 kilometers maybe it was. Go back in the back. There's a big, huge industrial complex that used to make freight-- freight cars in [NON-ENGLISH]. So we have no choice, really. There was no food, and we were in danger, they were in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one of their friends, a boy, lead us out to the highway. And that time, our clothes from [NON-ENGLISH] came in from-- civilian clothes was all fallen apart, so the Germans give us their working clothes, their [NON-ENGLISH], the one piece, you know? Like an overall.

speaker: interviewee
text: Overalls. And we had some hats from Germans, but not the insignias, or this kind of thing. And we spoke good German. So we paraded as Germans almost. Anyway, we came out on the highway. We spoke good German. That Jakob [NON-ENGLISH] picked up his finger and stop the trucks which was coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: They picked up soldiers, you know, hitchhiking on the way. And he stopped the truck and we asked the guy, where are you going? He said, I'm going to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was about 40 kilometers before Dneprodzerzhinsk. [NON-ENGLISH] is the huge [NON-ENGLISH] that the communists build it in 1935, I think it was. It was a huge piece of propaganda that the [NON-ENGLISH] was building that [NON-ENGLISH], like the hydroelectric station. So-- and on the way, the MP, so to speak, in English way, was stopping the trucks. But they didn't stop the truck to-- to-- to check what they're carrying. But they stopped if the driver had a license to go from here to there. Everything had to be documented. So they checked just the driver, and they didn't look in the back, and we went. So very often, they stopped the trucks on the way and we passed. We came into [NON-ENGLISH]. The guy said, that's it. You have to get off. We got off. He said, you look over there and maybe you get a truck to Dneprodzerzhinsk. So we're looking around. We found the truck to Dneprodzerzhinsk. Also, everything in German. We spoke in German and everything. They thought that we are Germans, but just dirty Germans that just came from work or something. So-- and the other driver who took us into the Dneprodzerzhinsk. Was afternoon. Afternoon, we came in into Dneprodzerzhinsk. And so many trucks, so many soldiers from different companies. Usually, the company occupied the town and this is the administration. So everybody's the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you have some tank units people, from-- from [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what else, telephone, these kind of things. So we start talking to the soldiers in the street. How did-- what happened? And we're get the, oh, they told us in Zaporozh'e that the Russians attacked nighttime. We hardly got away with our pants on. And a lot of them-- so-- and we start to find out other things. I say, how are you eating? What-- where are you sleeping? So they told us they're going to the [NON-ENGLISH], this is the chief of the military. And he gives them a slip of paper to go to a particular place where they're getting food. And they're having apartments that is to their disposition. So they gave address to the apartments. So they had where to sleep and where are they getting the food. We walked around. We are not Germans. What can we do? Just walking around like that, and you know, then we stop us for a paper. We had no papers, nothing-- nothing, without anything. This Jakob, [NON-ENGLISH], he looked like a German-- blond hair, blue eyes, and very like-- not aggressive, but he was-- you can see on his face he's not afraid. So I said, you know what? I'll Go into that [NON-ENGLISH], and I'm going to tell him a story that we are Poles. Because there was-- it was very common that Poles joined the Germans, helped them out in the-- as volunteers. But they were Poles. We're not Poles. So I said, I'm going to tell them that we are Poles. You two stay outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I'm not coming out, split, run wherever you can. But we were standing at the outside. I'm-- I'm in one place, the [NON-ENGLISH] was on the other side. Jakob comes out. And we saw a piece of paper that we were to get the-- the-- the food, and another was where we should go sleep. So he told us a story that that commandant said, look. If you would have been Germans, I can incorporate you in our-- in our other unit, and they're going to go to front, and you're going to be fighting like any other Germans. But since you are Poles, I cannot do that. Come back in 10 days, or seven days. They told us to come back. Maybe some of the company-- he describe us-- we should describe how the company-- what kind of logo the company had. Every company has a different logo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he made the logo, he made it upside down, you know? So they wouldn't have recognized it. He said, maybe somebody will come along and ask for you. So meantime, you have here food and you have where to sleep. Fine. He came out, and-- and-- and we got-- when he came out, we got together and we decide, what do we do now? We-- we decided not to go to sleep in the place where he send us. Because somebody may-- may-- may recognize us or something. And you say, OK, these guys are over there? Go get them. So we-- we went and got the food from that place, from the store. This was especially for soldiers. Civilians couldn't get in there. And we walk around with the food like that and walked down the street. There was an old lady staying there in front of the house. And we spoke to her in Russian. She said, oh, you-- how come you speak Russian? No, we are not Russian. We are Poles Oh, hi. So I don't remember. I ask her, the other guy her, can we have some food here? Can-- could you cook for us? She said, sure, why not? Come in. So she had two rooms, I think. We gave her the food, everything. And she cooked. She cooked for us and she ate with us together, whatever we had. And in a day or two or something like that, we had to go again and get some food. So this story was going on for a week, I think, or 10 days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then Jakob [NON-ENGLISH] went back there. And again, he said, look here. Nobody's asking for you. I cannot give you food all the time. You have to go to work. There is a industrial complex where they're manufacturing railroad cars. I'll-- I'll call up the major. There was a major there, the director. And I'll ask him if he needs some people. So I asked the qualifications.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we said, we all can drive a car, we work on [NON-ENGLISH], and all these kind of things. We're mechanics. So he called up the major down in the factory. He said, I have three people here, three Poles. And you have to employ them because I have-- I kept them, and this is it, if you have work for them. He said, sure, send them down.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave us a paper that these three Poles are-- are going to-- in case somebody stops us-- are going to-- to the-- to that factory to see Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] The director was Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] We came down. And he-- right way, he took the driver, took him on, as his personal driver. He had a [NON-ENGLISH], his personal driver, he told him, because in [NON-ENGLISH], he also spoke German. We all spoke German. So he was so glad, because his driver didn't speak German. He was dirty. Louses are crawling all over him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told them that my driver doesn't speak. He's dirty. He louses. He's loused up. So he took him right away in. You're going to be my personal driver-- fine. Me, he put in-- in a mechanical division. I should work there. And Jakob [NON-ENGLISH] put in a lathe, a lathe that turns the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the wheels for-- for the railroad, railroad wheels. It was a huge, huge lathe, I'd never seen that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, everybody had a job already. I was working in that-- in that mechanical [NON-ENGLISH], what work gave me, you know, a locomotive has two cylinders, steam cylinders. So I had to bring it down. The tolerance-- the cover of the cylinder should be zero. Because in the cylinders, is a-- there's a tremendous pressure-- steam pressure. Imagine, two cylinders are pulling a whole train with 150 railroad cars. Has to be some pressure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there couldn't be anything in between. So I had to bring down that thing to zero level that they should match each other. So every time I needed to sharpen that-- that-- that instrument, they used to come along, this-- this-- this neighbor, he sharpened for me, because he was afraid. And everybody was working. We found out later what happened. I'll tell you later that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was working, and every-- I didn't think that I needed to watch me. I didn't think I needed to helped me, so helpful. And I had cigarettes. We got from the-- from the-- what the Germans gave us, the food, we had cigarettes, too. Because, you know, in the army, they give you cigarettes. So I used to give them cigarettes. And they were so glad, so happy. And we worked there. And then we had to look for an apartment. Because to live in that old lady's house, it wasn't comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have to look for-- for an apartment. So the guy next door, I spoke to him in-- in Russian. And they were suspecting us. Now it comes out. They were suspected us that the Germans put in a spy there to watch the rest of them-- how they work, what they're doing, what all-- Let me clarify this. They were suspecting you of being Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- no, being a spy for the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Being a spy for the Germans. But they weren't suspecting you of being Jews yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, no. I-- I-- I told them that I'm-- I'm Pole, Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, I told this guy next to me which was so helpful, young. So I told him, look here. You know any apartment that-- for the three of us that we-- we'd like to have it where to sleep over. Because we used to have an apartment. It wasn't too comfortable.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, sure. I have a-- I can give you a-- a room. So we went over to his-- to his house, and he gave us a room. And we fixed up everything. Everybody had a bed. And we had-- it was more modern. We had a toilet in the room, and water was running. Next door was a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is a home away from home for the soldiers. You go in there, you get a meal, and you can sleep over. The front line soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we worked-- in the factory, we worked eight hours-- eight hours or 10 hours, whatever. And afterward, we went home and we were free people. So after work, while we were working there, the German-- that-- that major, director of the factory, took-- took a man. That was a-- a-- a magazine-- a warehouse with uniforms, German uniforms. They had the uniforms in the thousands of them, everything-- uniforms, boots, everything. So he took him on there, his driver, that he should look like a human being. So he told Hans over there, the guy, he should give him a uniform. So he gave him a uniform and took us there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We should, you know, look decent. He gave us a uniform, too. That's how we got in in the German uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were already 100% Germans. After work, we put on that uniform, and we walked out in the street-- it was summertime-- looking for girls, looking for-- we did not associate with any of the civilian people. Because to them, it's, you know, the Ukraine. They had that nose. They could smell you that you were a Jew. But with us, like, we went into the barber. And the barber, heil Hitler, you know. Shave me around. There used to be a style that shaved around the head close by with the shaving. And they were scared of us-- Germans, no?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we associate only with the-- with the German soldiers. We found in the street German soldiers. They were walking the streets just like us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What name were you going by at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaplanski.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] was Takrovski and Laske is a German name. So he went on his name. He told them a story that his mother is-- is German. His father was Polish. You know, you try to do everything that remain alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were walking the street. And we had already soldiers that we were associate with them. Every day after work, you know, we got together.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to look for girls, and we went into that place in that home away from home for soldiers. We went in, heil Hitler, you know, the whole schmiel. And we sit down. We ate and walked out. And everybody thought that we-- that we are Germans. One day, [NON-ENGLISH] went to-- had to drive our the chef of-- I mean, that major, engineer of that plant. Had to drive him to [NON-ENGLISH] back where we changed-- changed trucks. So he had to drive him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was in uniform, everything fine-- nice and clean. While he was walking up the steps, the major had to go to some-- some-- some German institution to attend something. While [NON-ENGLISH] was walking up the steps, who you think walked against him? A guy from our company that we-- we escaped. But he didn't recognize him-- lucky. All right, [NON-ENGLISH] came back. He said, do you know whom I met? That fat guy, the big one. Not-- all right, we got away with it. One day came a truck in to be fixed. Because as I told you, they were fixing trucks and all these kind of things. One day came in a truck in to be fixed. And you know who came? A guy from the company that we escaped. We were in hot water. So we disappeared. He came to the-- to the mechanical division where I worked with his truck. Because there had to be some welding done. Something got loose in the truck. So he came there. So then I says, I saw him coming in. I walked out the other door. And then I gave him a message that I had the stomach ache, I don't feel good. So [NON-ENGLISH] and Jakob [NON-ENGLISH], they watched him already. So when he left, I-- I-- I came back. So we had a close brush with our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it comes to the-- in the-- we used to go out with girls and with-- with girls and with the--
